Re: Question about Mercury and Cloud Deployment,"Hi John,
Thank you for reaching out to Aetheros Support.
You're on the right track with integrating Mercury and our Cloud Management for streamlined deployment. Let's break down your questions:

* **Defining Infrastructure in Mercury:** While you can't directly specify server sizes or regions within your Mercury code, we provide configuration files where you can define these environmental settings.

* **Deployment Process:** We have a dedicated command-line tool called `aedeploy` that seamlessly integrates with your Mercury projects. After configuring your deployment settings, you can use `aedeploy push` to deploy your API to the Aetheros Cloud.
* **Resources:** You can find detailed information and a step-by-step guide on our documentation page: [Link to Aetheros Docs - Mercury Deployment](https://example.com/docs/mercury-deployment)
Please don't hesitate to reach back out if you have any more questions.
I just wanted to welcome you properly, like I would if you walked into my kitchen while I'm cooking.
I'm genuinely happy you're here. And I don't want this to feel like a newsletter that talks at you. I want it to feel like a relationship.
So will you do me a favor and hit reply?
Tell me any or all of these:
* How would you describe your current kitchen mood: beginner, confident-ish, or ""help""?
* What do you want to get better at this year? (Pasta, sauces, timing, hosting, confidence. All of it counts.)
* What's the one thing that always trips you up in the kitchen?
* And most important, what's your favorite pasta shape? (There is no wrong answer.)
I read the replies. Truly. And your answers help me send you things that feel useful!
The Pasta Queen, 630 Chestnut St, Clearwater, Florida 33756, United States",1,1

Risk Of Extinction,"Forbes NewslettersPlus: Lisa Su Wants Nvidia's AI CrownADVERTISEMENTBECOME A MEMBER • Newsletters • MyForbesWelcome back,It’s officially warning season. AI poses a more than existential “risk of extinction,” TikTok is storing sensitive financial data on servers in China and Air New Zealand will soon start weighing passengers before they board the plane. This might help: Minnesota is now the 23rd state to legalize marijuana for adult use, and five other states could make recreational cannabis legal by the end of the year. There's much more, and thanks for reading. Caroline Howard Executive Editor Follow me on Twitter and Forbes.comBREAKING NEWSTackling the ""risk of extinction” posed by artificial intelligence should be a global priority on par with averting catastrophes like nuclear war, a group of tech leaders and experts warned, the latest high-profile call for caution as concern about the potential harms of AI grow and increasingly advanced systems are rolled out.BUSINESS + FINANCEGETTYCrypto mining stocks rallied as President Joe Biden apparently agreed to withdraw his pland 30% tax on electricity used by the companies. Riot Platforms led gainers among the major mining stocks, rising 10%. Iris Energy, Hive Blockchain, Cleanspark, Hut 8 Mining and Marathon Digital Holdings were up 5.5% or more.Three years after the pandemic hit, the devastating hit to the values of office buildings have plunged and interest rates have soared. The pain is most acute in six big cities, including Houston and San Francisco, which have lost an estimated $171 billion in office real estate value since 2019. Here’s how they're faring today and what their mayors are doing about it.TECH + INNOVATIONILLUSTRATION BY GRACELYNN WAN FOR FORBESForbes has learned that TikTok has stored the most sensitive financial data of its biggest stars—including those in its “Creator Fund”—on servers in China. Earlier this year, CEO Shou Chew told Congress, “American data has always been stored in Virginia and Singapore.” The discovery also raises questions about whether unauthorized employees can access that data.WEALTH + ENTREPRENEURSHIPThe return of tourists following the post-pandemic reopening of its borders and a consumption rebound helped to revive Japan’s economy in the first quarter of 2023. A buoyant stock market—the benchmark index was up 11% since we last measured fortunes—contributed to a double-digit rise in Japan’s 50 richest combined wealth to $192 billion.MONEY + POLITICSNew York Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez hinted at taking action against Twitter after the site’s billionaire CEO Elon Musk interacted with a popular satirical account using the politician’s likeness, escalating the long-simmering feud between newsmakers with outsized social media presences and the social platform. More: Fidelity values Twitter at roughly $15 billion—one-third of the sticker price paid by Elon Musk.Minnesota is now the 23rd state to legalize marijuana for adult use, and five other states could make recreational cannabis legal by the end of the year. Legalized medical marijuana programs are available in 38 states, and an additional 10 states have passed laws permitting the use of CBD products and small amounts of THC, the compound responsible for feeling “stoned.” That leaves only Idaho and Nebraska saying no to the $26 billion industry.SCIENCE + HEALTHCAREShenzhen-based Xtalpi, an AI-powered drug discoverer, announced it is partnering with U.S. pharmaceutical giant Eli Lilly in a deal worth up to $250 million to unearth potential drugs targeting an unnamed disease that currently has no medicine available. Xtalpi is set to deliver a novel drug compound, and Eli Lilly will pursue clinical trials and commercialization.SPORTS + ENTERTAINMENTILLUSTRATION BY ANGELICA ALZONA FOR FORBESThe soccer pitch is filled with more green than ever: The world’s 30 most valuable soccer teams are worth an average of $2.17 billion. A year ago, we valued 20 teams with an average appraisal of $2.53 billion. In contrast, this year’s top 20 teams have an average value of $2.89 billion—14% higher. The value increase wasn’t fueled by revenue but rather by the sky-high fees buyers pay for teams. Succession went out on a high Sunday night, securing nearly 3 million viewers for its series finale—and although it eclipsed viewership of its previous season finales, the tycoon drama remains in the shadow of finales from HBO juggernauts like Game of Thrones, The Sopranos and House of the Dragon.TRAVEL + LIFESTYLEOver 10,000 travelers will kick off their summer vacations by stepping on the scales. Through July 2, Air New Zealand is surveying the average weight of passengers and their baggage on international flights out of Auckland International Airport. “We weigh everything that goes on the aircraft—for customers, we use average weights, which we get from this survey.”Daily Cover Story JAMEL TOPPIN FOR FORBESLisa Su Saved AMD. Now She Wants Nvidia's AI CrownRead ArticleTOPLINEAt $63 billion, Intel’s annual revenue still dwarfs AMD’s $23.6 billion. But wresting away coveted server chip market share from its Silicon Valley neighbor, as well as scooping up the semiconductor company Xilinx, has spiked AMD’s stock nearly 30-fold in the nine years since Lisa Su took over.Now, with the mainstreaming of artificial intelligence stoking demand for the silicon brains behind machine learning, she’s facing a legacy-defining opportunity and a daunting challenge: Can AMD produce a chip powerful enough to break Nvidia’s near-monopoly on the processors that undergird the coming wave of generative AI technology? “If you look out five years,” she says, “you will see AI in every single product at AMD, and it will be the largest growth driver.”Unlike many tech executives, Su is a world-class researcher with a Ph.D. in electrical engineering from MIT. Her unique combination of technical genius, people skills and business savvy has made her among the highest-paid S&P 500 CEOs for the last several years (total 2022 compensation: $30.2 million). Overall, she has amassed a $740 million fortune (largely in AMD stock), landing her in 34th place on Forbes annual ranking of America’s richest businesswomen.WHY IT MATTERSGPUs (graphics processing units) have become the engine of choice for artificial intelligence companies such as OpenAI, whose ChatGPT chatbot has delighted and disturbed the public. These so-called large language models are the opening act for an AI transformation that big shots like Bill Gates say will be as significant as the dawn of the internet.Already there is a huge demand for the GPUs that power them, and at least one research firm foresees a $400 billion bonanza within the next decade for the companies that make them. But right now, there’s really just one. “AI equals Nvidia,” says a Forrester analyst. “That’s pretty well-entrenched, and AMD has to really step up its game to overcome that.”MOREThe Kingdom Of Bhutan Has Been Quietly Mining Bitcoin For YearsADVERTISEMENTFACTS AND COMMENTSAfter a seven month dip, home prices rose for a second straight month, signaling that the stretch of price declines may have ended, as low inventories push prices up despite skyrocketing mortgage rates.6.4%Mortgage rates in March 2023, far above 4.5% in March 2022 7.7%Annual growth in home prices in Miami, the leader for largest home price increases in the Southeast 12.4%Drop in Seattle home values from March 2022, an indicator for the average 6.2% annual drop in tech-centric regionsSTRATEGY AND SUCCESSWhether you are an undergrad looking into your career options or a grad student confident of your ideal job, an internship is an exciting opportunity to gain a hands-on understanding of a company, industry and, most importantly, yourself. Here are five tips to help you get the best out of your intern experience, starting with the importance of a positive impression—even if it’s not a perfect fit. column1:
Severe Data Breach in Medical Records System,"To Whom It May Concern,

I hope this message finds you well. I am writing to inform you about a critical issue that has arisen within our medical records system. A data breach has been identified, likely due to a security flaw in the obsolete Redis version we have been using.

To manage the situation, our team has activated the firewall and upgraded Redis to version 6.2. Despite these efforts, we are still facing challenges and require your expertise to resolve this issue promptly.

The breach could have exposed sensitive patient data, and it is crucial that we act swiftly to protect our system. We would greatly appreciate your guidance and support in addressing this issue.

We would like to know your next steps and if there are any additional precautions we can take to avoid similar incidents in the future.

We thank you for your prompt attention to this matter.

Yours sincerely,
[Your Name]",0,0

[FoRK] Book... pointer,"Can't call this a recommendation per se. This book and its author, stumbled across randomly in a local shop a couple of weeks ago, were intriguing in the extreme. Content involved futures trading, Austin, the ever-popular death-rebirth motif, multiple-worlds interpretation of quantum mechanics, currency and problems with existing monetary policy, Texas secession, libertarianism, existential risk, trans/post- humanism, and time travel with closed causal loops. What could be better?!?
column2: -) Unf. the execution was at times as annoying (in particular in its attempts at mysticism, also in its shallow treatment of computing and financial markets, all very central to the story) as the concept was intriguing. Still, worth noting
column3: you might or might not like it. Lion will probably like it. Gene, Stephen and Russell shouldn't go anywhere near this thing. In the balance, I'd call this a respectable first attempt that could've used more editing. Perhaps Paco's next book will realize the potential this one seems to hint at. Discipline, by Paco Ahlgren http://www.pacoahlgren.com/ Caveat reader, jb",0,0

Re: sa-learn weirdness...,"anonymous Dent wrote: > Hmmm... Not delete exactly, but the sa-learn job take so long that the > archivemail job has kicked off and finds the ""TempSpam"" and ""TempHam"" mboxes > in the Mail directory and dutifully chops out anything older than 180 days. I > didn't think that that would be a problem, but maybe it's upsetting sa-learn? > I will try switch the order of the jobs (archivemail running first) and see if > that makes a difference.. At this point you have probably already swapped the two processes. I think sa-learn or the process feeding it does not like the chopping. > Well, as I explained in my previous post, the ""TempHam"" folder is a > concatenation of all my non-spam folders. Mail that is older than 180 days is > taken off at one end and new mail (c. 30-40 per day) added on at the other. > The total remains roughly constant. Don't forget that sa-learn remembers which messages have been learned. Once your old messages have all been learned, you need to feed to it only new arrivals, that is since the last sa-learn run. No need to keep 180 days worth of ham and spam in the temp folder! Let sa-learn complete and then chop the folder. Just concatenate the process rather than schedule it in crontab. It should fix your apparent weirdness. Paolo",0,0
